content:
First, fix coordinates so that the line \(A\) is the \(x\)\n–axis and the center of the big circle \(B\) is at \((0,R)\) with \(R=2008\).  Every circle \(C_n\) is tangent to the \(x\)\n–axis, so if its radius is \(r_n\) its center is \((x_n,r_n)\).  We also require that \(C_n\) be tangent to \(B\) and to \(C_{n-1}\).  

It is extremely convenient to use Descartes’ “kissing‐circle” theorem in the form of curvatures (signed curvatures, where a line is curvature \(0\), a circle of radius \(r\) externally tangent to the rest is curvature \(+1/r\)).  Denote by
\[
k_B=\frac1{2008},\quad k_{n}=\frac1{r_n},\quad k_{\rm line}=0.
\]
Given three pairwise‐tangent “circles” of curvatures \(k_{\rm line},\,k_B,\,k_{n-1}\), the fourth mutually tangent circle has curvature
\[
k_n \;=\; k_{\rm line}+k_B+k_{n-1}\;\pm\;2\sqrt{k_{\rm line}\,k_B+k_B\,k_{n-1}+k_{n-1}\,k_{\rm line}}
\,.
\]
Here \(k_{\rm line}=0\), so
\[
k_n \;=\; k_{n-1}+k_B\;\pm\;2\sqrt{k_B\,k_{n-1}}\,.
\]
The “\(+\)” sign gives the small‐circle‐chain which tends to zero radius; but the problem tells us \(r_1=1\) and then \(r_2>1\), so we must take the “\(-\)” sign.  Thus
\[
k_n
=
k_{n-1}+k_B-2\sqrt{k_B\,k_{n-1}}
\;=\;
\Bigl(\sqrt{k_{n-1}}-\sqrt{k_B}\Bigr)^2.
\]
Set \(x=\sqrt{k_B}=1/\sqrt{2008}\).  Since \(k_1=1\), we get by induction
\[
\sqrt{k_n}
=\sqrt{k_1}-(n-1)\,x
=1-(n-1)x,
\]
hence
\[
k_n=\bigl(1-(n-1)x\bigr)^2
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
r_n=\frac1{k_n}=\frac1{\bigl(1-(n-1)x\bigr)^2}\,.
\]
In order for \(r_n\) to stay positive and finite we need
\[
1-(n-1)x>0
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
n-1<\frac1{x}=\sqrt{2008}
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
n<1+\sqrt{2008}\approx45.82.
\]
Hence the largest integer \(n\) for which the construction still makes sense is
\[
\boxed{45}.
\]

message.content:

\[
k_n \;=\; k_{\rm line}+k_B+k_{n-1}\;\pm\;2\sqrt{k_{\rm line}\,k_B+k_B\,k_{n-1}+k_{n-1}\,k_{\rm line}}
\,.

\Bigl(\sqrt{k_{n-1}}-\sqrt{k_B}\Bigr)^2.

\[
\sqrt{k_n}
=\sqrt{k_1}-(n-1)\,x
=1-(n-1)x,

\[
\boxed{45}.
